---
title: "Brenda Dervin's Sense-Making Methodology"
subtitle: "A Practitioner's Guide — Theory, Method, and Applied Analytics"
author: "Frederic Labadie"
date: last-modified
format:
  html:
    theme: [cosmo, custom.scss]
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-location: left
    number-sections: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-fold: false
    anchor-sections: true
    callout-appearance: minimal
    grid:
      sidebar-width: 260px
      body-width: 760px
  pdf:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    geometry: margin=2.5cm
    fontsize: 11pt
    linestretch: 1.4
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{microtype}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

```{=html}
<style>
/* ── Typography ── */
@import url('https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,500;0,700;1,500&family=Source+Serif+4:ital,wght@0,300;0,400;0,600;1,300;1,400&family=JetBrains+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap');

body { font-family: 'Source Serif 4', Georgia, serif; font-size: 16px; line-height: 1.8; color: #1a1a1a; }
h1, h2, h3, h4 { font-family: 'Playfair Display', Georgia, serif; font-weight: 700; }
h1 { font-size: 2rem; border-bottom: 2px solid #0d6e56; padding-bottom: .4rem; }
h2 { font-size: 1.45rem; margin-top: 2rem; }
h3 { font-size: 1.15rem; color: #2a4a40; }
code, pre, .sourceCode { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; }
.quarto-title-meta { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .75rem; letter-spacing: .08em; color: #888; }

/* ── Concept blocks ── */
.concept-box {
  background: #f7f9f8;
  border-left: 3px solid #0d6e56;
  padding: 14px 18px;
  border-radius: 0 8px 8px 0;
  margin: 16px 0;
}
.concept-box.gap  { border-color: #6d3acd; background: #f5f3ff; }
.concept-box.bridge { border-color: #b94a28; background: #fdf2ef; }
.concept-box.outcome { border-color: #8a6c0e; background: #fdf8ec; }
.concept-label {
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
  font-size: .65rem;
  letter-spacing: .15em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  color: #0d6e56;
  display: block;
  margin-bottom: 4px;
}
.concept-box.gap .concept-label { color: #6d3acd; }
.concept-box.bridge .concept-label { color: #b94a28; }
.concept-box.outcome .concept-label { color: #8a6c0e; }
.concept-title { font-weight: 700; font-size: 1rem; color: #111; display: block; margin-bottom: 4px; }

/* ── Diagram boxes ── */
.diagram-box {
  background: #0f1612;
  border-radius: 10px;
  padding: 24px 28px;
  margin: 20px 0;
  overflow-x: auto;
}
.diagram-box pre {
  color: #b0c4b8;
  font-size: .72rem;
  line-height: 1.75;
  margin: 0;
  white-space: pre;
}
.d-teal  { color: #4ecba0; }
.d-amber { color: #e8c97a; }
.d-coral { color: #e8927a; }
.d-green { color: #8acea8; }
.d-dim   { color: #445a50; }

/* ── Flashcards ── */
.fc-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(280px, 1fr)); gap: 14px; margin: 20px 0; }
.fc { perspective: 800px; height: 130px; cursor: pointer; }
.fc-inner { position: relative; width: 100%; height: 100%; transform-style: preserve-3d; transition: transform .45s ease; }
.fc.flipped .fc-inner { transform: rotateY(180deg); }
.fc-front, .fc-back { position: absolute; inset: 0; backface-visibility: hidden; border-radius: 8px; padding: 14px 16px; display: flex; flex-direction: column; justify-content: space-between; }
.fc-front { background: #fff; border: 1px solid #d4d0c8; border-left: 3px solid #0d6e56; }
.fc-back  { background: #0f1612; transform: rotateY(180deg); }
.fc-num { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .6rem; letter-spacing: .12em; color: #aaa; }
.fc-q { font-size: .85rem; font-weight: 600; color: #111; line-height: 1.4; }
.fc-a { font-size: .8rem; color: #b0c4b8; line-height: 1.4; }
.fc-hint { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .6rem; color: #4ecba0; }
.fc-back .fc-hint { color: #2a4a40; }

/* ── Test questions ── */
details.tq { background: #fff; border: 1px solid #d4d0c8; border-radius: 8px; margin: 10px 0; overflow: hidden; }
details.tq summary { padding: 14px 16px; font-weight: 600; font-size: .9rem; cursor: pointer; display: grid; grid-template-columns: 36px 1fr; gap: 10px; align-items: center; list-style: none; }
details.tq summary::-webkit-details-marker { display: none; }
.tq-num { background: #f0ede5; border-radius: 50%; width: 30px; height: 30px; display: flex; align-items: center; justify-content: center; font-family: 'Playfair Display', serif; font-size: 1rem; font-weight: 700; color: #888; }
details.tq[open] summary { border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; }
.tq-body { padding: 14px 16px 14px 62px; font-size: .88rem; color: #3d3d3d; line-height: 1.6; }

/* ── Scenario cards ── */
.scenario { background: #fff; border: 1px solid #d4d0c8; border-radius: 10px; margin: 16px 0; overflow: hidden; }
.scenario-head { background: #f4f1eb; padding: 12px 16px; border-bottom: 1px solid #d4d0c8; display: flex; gap: 12px; align-items: center; }
.scenario-num { font-family: 'Playfair Display', serif; font-size: 1.8rem; font-weight: 700; color: #ccc; line-height: 1; }
.scenario-domain { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .6rem; letter-spacing: .15em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #0d6e56; }
.scenario-title { font-weight: 700; font-size: .95rem; color: #111; }
.scenario-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 0; }
.scenario-cell { padding: 12px 14px; border-right: 1px solid #eee; border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; }
.scenario-cell:last-child, .scenario-cell:nth-child(even) { border-right: none; }
.scenario-cell-label { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .6rem; letter-spacing: .15em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #0d6e56; margin-bottom: 4px; display: block; }
.scenario-cell p { font-size: .82rem; margin: 0; color: #555; line-height: 1.45; }

/* ── Ethics cards ── */
.ethics-card { background: #fff; border: 1px solid #d4d0c8; border-radius: 10px; margin: 16px 0; overflow: hidden; }
.ethics-head { background: #f4f1eb; padding: 12px 16px; border-bottom: 1px solid #d4d0c8; }
.ethics-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 0; }
.ethics-cell { padding: 12px 14px; border-right: 1px solid #eee; }
.ethics-cell:last-child { border-right: none; }
.ethics-label-risk { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .6rem; letter-spacing: .15em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #c0392b; margin-bottom: 6px; display: block; }
.ethics-label-mit { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .6rem; letter-spacing: .15em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #0d6e56; margin-bottom: 6px; display: block; }
.ethics-cell li { font-size: .82rem; color: #555; line-height: 1.45; margin-bottom: 4px; }

/* ── Day plan ── */
.day-grid { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(7, 1fr); gap: 8px; margin: 16px 0; }
.day-card { background: #fff; border: 1px solid #d4d0c8; border-radius: 8px; overflow: hidden; }
.day-head { background: #0f1612; padding: 8px 6px; text-align: center; }
.day-n { font-family: 'Playfair Display', serif; font-size: 1.5rem; font-weight: 700; color: #4ecba0; line-height: 1; display: block; }
.day-name { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .55rem; letter-spacing: .08em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #445a50; }
.day-body { padding: 8px 8px; }
.day-theme { font-size: .75rem; font-weight: 700; color: #111; margin-bottom: 5px; }
.day-task { font-size: .65rem; color: #666; line-height: 1.35; padding: 2px 0; border-bottom: 1px solid #f0ede5; }
.day-task::before { content: '—'; color: #0d6e56; margin-right: 3px; }

/* ── Fieldwork template ── */
.field-box { background: #fffef7; border: 1px solid #d4d0c8; border-radius: 10px; padding: 20px 24px; margin: 20px 0; }
.field-row { margin-bottom: 20px; }
.field-label { font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .65rem; letter-spacing: .18em; text-transform: uppercase; color: #0d6e56; display: block; margin-bottom: 6px; }
.field-prompt { font-size: .85rem; color: #888; font-style: italic; margin-bottom: 4px; }
.field-line { width: 100%; border: none; border-bottom: 1px solid #d4d0c8; padding: 6px 2px; font-family: 'Source Serif 4', serif; font-size: .9rem; background: transparent; color: #111; outline: none; display: block; margin-bottom: 4px; }

/* ── Comparison table override ── */
table.compare { width: 100%; border-collapse: collapse; margin: 16px 0; font-size: .85rem; }
table.compare thead th { background: #0f1612; color: #b0c4b8; font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .65rem; letter-spacing: .1em; text-transform: uppercase; padding: 10px 12px; text-align: left; border: none; }
table.compare tbody tr:nth-child(odd) { background: #fff; }
table.compare tbody tr:nth-child(even) { background: #f7f9f8; }
table.compare td { padding: 9px 12px; border-bottom: 1px solid #eee; vertical-align: top; color: #444; }
table.compare td:first-child { font-weight: 600; color: #0d6e56; font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace; font-size: .75rem; }

/* ── Print overrides ── */
@media print {
  .fc-grid, .fc { display: none; }
  .fc-grid + * { margin-top: 0; }
  details.tq { border: 1px solid #ccc; }
  details.tq summary::after { content: ' [see below]'; font-style: italic; color: #888; }
  .tq-body { display: block !important; }
  .day-grid { grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr); }
}
</style>
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**How to use this document**

- **As a website** → render with `quarto render smm_guide.qmd --to html`
- **As a printable PDF** → render with `quarto render smm_guide.qmd --to pdf`
- **As a fieldwork handbook** → print Section 10 (Fieldwork Template) separately
- **Flashcards** → click to flip in HTML; both Q and A print automatically in PDF
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**Why I built this**

Dervin's Sense-Making Methodology is the qualitative counterpart to the quantitative analytics work I do every day. The telemetry tells me *what* happened — where users dropped off, which variant won, which segment converts. SMM gives me the framework for understanding *why* — what gap the user was experiencing, what bridge they were trying to construct, and why the product did or didn't help them across it.

I couldn't find a resource with the theoretical rigour intact that was also practically oriented toward the work I actually do: analytics, UX telemetry, A/B testing, measurement planning. So I built one.

The talk this connects to: **Finding the Hypothesis When You Only Know the Question** — on the relationship between quantitative analytics and qualitative gap diagnosis.

*Frederic Labadie — Senior Data Analyst & Analytics Architect, Amsterdam*
:::

---

# Core Class {#core}

## Origins & Intellectual History

Brenda Dervin began developing SMM in the early 1970s during doctoral and early faculty work at Ohio State University, driven by a persistent empirical failure: public information campaigns designed to change behaviour consistently failed to reach target audiences. The dominant explanation — "they didn't receive the message" — seemed inadequate. Dervin's diagnosis: the transmission model itself was wrong.

| Influence | What Dervin Borrowed | What She Transformed |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Shannon-Weaver | Communication as process | Information as a fixed object that transfers intact |
| Carter's info-seeking | Users have genuine needs | Needs as static, predictable, topic-based |
| Phenomenology (Schutz) | Experience is situated, temporal | Observer-independent "reality" |
| Constructivism (Kelly) | Knowledge is constructed, not received | Learning as passive absorption |
| Critical theory | Power shapes information access | Value-free, neutral information science |
| Feminist epistemology | Standpoint and subjectivity matter | Universal, disembodied "objectivity" |

## Seven Philosophical Assumptions

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal" icon=false}
These are not optional background — they are constitutive of the methodology.
:::

1. **Reality is discontinuous.** Gaps are not exceptions to be explained away — they are the fundamental condition of human experience.

2. **Information is constructed, not found.** There are no free-standing information objects. Information is always constructed in the act of bridging a gap in a specific situation.

3. **Situation is constitutive, not contextual.** The specific time-space context determines what counts as a gap, what bridges are available, and what outcomes are possible.

4. **People are active sense-makers, not passive receivers.** The human being is the agent constructing bridges using internal resources (memory, emotion, knowledge) and external resources (information, people, systems).

5. **Universal procedure; unique instance.** Everyone sense-makes (universal), but the specific sense made is irreducibly unique to that person's situation and moment.

6. **Subjectivity is datum, not noise.** Confusion, emotion, and intuition are what SMM is designed to capture — not artefacts to be controlled for.

7. **Both internal and external orders are real.** Neither pure subjectivism nor pure objectivism is adequate. SMM operates in the space between.

## Key Concepts

```{=html}
<div class="concept-box">
  <span class="concept-label">Concept 01</span>
  <span class="concept-title">Situation</span>
  The specific time-space context in which a person finds themselves. Situations create, constrain, and define the sense-making that is possible. Not mere background — situation is the operative <em>ground</em> of all sense-making activity.
</div>

<div class="concept-box gap">
  <span class="concept-label">Concept 02</span>
  <span class="concept-title">Gap</span>
  The discontinuity that interrupts forward movement — a question, confusion, barrier, uncertainty, or absence. Not a failure of the person: the engine of sense-making. Without a gap, no sense-making is needed.
</div>

<div class="concept-box bridge">
  <span class="concept-label">Concept 03</span>
  <span class="concept-title">Bridge</span>
  The sense constructed to cross the gap. Bridges may be ideas, information found, memories recalled, emotional resolutions, social connections, or procedural steps. Bridges are <em>built</em>, not received. They may succeed, fail, or generate new gaps.
</div>

<div class="concept-box outcome">
  <span class="concept-label">Concept 04</span>
  <span class="concept-title">Outcome / Use</span>
  What results from bridging. Outcomes include: helps (movement continues), hurts (movement blocked), new gaps (created by the bridge), and specific uses — deciding, acting, connecting, evaluating, feeling. Multiple uses can follow one bridge.
</div>
```

## Gap Taxonomy

Dervin developed a rich taxonomy of gap types. Rather than asking users "what information do you need?" (presupposing topic-based needs), SMM asks about the *nature of the discontinuity*.

| Gap Type | Description | Typical Interview Signal |
|----------|-------------|--------------------------|
| Decision | Facing a choice; unclear how to decide | "I needed to choose between..." |
| Barrier | External obstacle blocking movement | "I couldn't get access to..." |
| Problematic | Something wrong but unclear what | "Something felt off but I couldn't name it..." |
| Role | Unclear how to behave in a role/situation | "I didn't know what I was supposed to do..." |
| Spin-out | Total disorientation; no clear question | "I had no idea where to start..." |
| Washout | Information received but doesn't address the gap | "I got a lot but none of it helped with what I needed..." |

## Sense-Making vs. Sense-Unmaking

In her later work (primarily 1990s onward), Dervin added **sense-unmaking** — recognition that people must also *dismantle* existing sense structures.

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
Sense-unmaking is not the failure of sense-making. It is a constitutive part of it. People must forget, unlearn, challenge assumptions, and destabilize established understandings before new sense can be made.

Sense-unmaking is particularly critical for: transformative learning, organizational change, critical consciousness, and creative work.
:::

## SMM vs. the Transmission Model

| Dimension | Transmission Model | Sense-Making Methodology |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Ontology of information | Information exists independently as an objective thing | Information is constructed in the act of bridging — always relational |
| Role of the human | Passive receiver / decoder | Active sense-maker constructing bridges |
| Communication model | Linear: Source → Message → Channel → Receiver | Recursive: Situation → Gap → Bridge → Outcome → New Situation |
| Unit of analysis | The message or information object | The gap: the specific discontinuity in a specific situation |
| Success criterion | Accurate transmission: did the message arrive intact? | Effective bridging: did the person cross the gap in a way that helped? |
| Role of context | Noise to be reduced | Constitutive: determines what a gap is and what bridges are possible |

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Dervin's core critique:** The transmission model assumes meaning inheres in messages — that the same message means the same thing to all receivers. SMM demonstrates empirically this is false. Designing for information transfer is designing for failure.
:::

---

# Visual Model {#visual}

## The Root Metaphor

The entire framework flows from a single spatial metaphor: a person moving through time-space encounters a gap — an absence, a question, a barrier. To continue the journey they must construct a bridge. What happens after they cross (or fail to cross) shapes their next situation.

```{=html}
<div class="diagram-box">
<pre>
  <span class="d-teal">SITUATION</span>          <span class="d-amber">GAP</span>              <span class="d-coral">BRIDGE</span>            <span class="d-green">OUTCOME</span>
  ─────────          ───              ──────            ───────
  Where you       What stops        What you          What results
  are now         movement          construct         from crossing

  [Person in]         ???           [Ideas found]     [Helps: moves
  [time-space]       /   \          [Info sought]      forward     ]
                    /     \         [Memories]        [Hurts: blocks]
                   /       \        [Feelings]        [New gap: loops]
                  /         \       [Connections]

  TIME ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────►</pre>
</div>
```

## The Formal S-G-B-O Diagram

```{=html}
<div class="diagram-box">
<pre>
                   <span class="d-amber">[ PERSON ]</span>
                  /            \
                 /  standing on  \
                /  edge of gap   \
               /                  \
  <span class="d-teal">SITUATION ──►</span>  · · · <span class="d-amber">GAP</span> · · ·  <span class="d-teal">──►</span>  <span class="d-coral">BRIDGE  ──►</span>  <span class="d-green">OUTCOME</span>
  [What I know]    [What I need]    [What helps]     [Use / Effect]
  [Where I am]     [What stops me]  [Constructed]    [New Situation]


  SENSE-UNMAKING adds a recursive arc:

  <span class="d-green">OUTCOME</span> ─────► may destabilize existing sense ─────► <span class="d-amber">NEW GAP</span>
                                                           │
                  Old bridge dismantled ◄──────────────────┘
                       <span class="d-dim">(sense-unmaking)</span></pre>
</div>
```

## The Recursive Loop

SMM is not a linear pipeline. Each outcome creates a new situation (or transforms the current one), generating new gaps. Crucially, some bridges *worsen* the situation — the model is not teleologically optimistic.

```{=html}
<div class="diagram-box">
<pre>
  S ──► G ──► B ──► O <span class="d-green">(help)</span>    ──► <span class="d-teal">New Situation S'</span>
  S ──► G ──► B ──► O <span class="d-coral">(hurt)</span>    ──► <span class="d-coral">Worse Situation S–</span>
  S ──► G ──► B ──► O <span class="d-amber">(new gap)</span> ──► <span class="d-amber">Spiral: new G within S</span>

  Multiple simultaneous gaps:
  S ──► G₁ <span class="d-dim">(decision)</span>  ──► B₁ ──► O₁
     ──► G₂ <span class="d-dim">(barrier)</span>   ──► B₂ ──► O₂   ← all active at once
     ──► G₃ <span class="d-dim">(confusion)</span> ──► B₃ ──► O₃</pre>
</div>
```

## Gap & Bridge Surface Signals

| Signal Type | Linguistic Markers | Indicates |
|-------------|-------------------|-----------|
| Questions | "I didn't know why..." / "I wondered how..." | GAP |
| Confusions | "It didn't make sense..." / "I was lost..." | GAP |
| Barriers | "I couldn't get through..." / "They wouldn't let me..." | GAP |
| Spin-out | "Everything was unclear..." / "I had no idea..." | GAP |
| External resource | Found a document / asked a person / used a tool | BRIDGE |
| Internal resource | Remembered something / drew on experience / felt calmer | BRIDGE |
| Social resource | Got help from someone / connected with a community | BRIDGE |
| Procedural resource | Followed a process / applied a rule / used a system | BRIDGE |

---

# Methodology in Practice {#method}

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**Core research commitment:** SMM research asks participants about *verbs*, not nouns. Not "what topic did you need information about?" but "what question were you trying to answer?" This verb-centered stance is what distinguishes SMM from topic-based information-seeking research.
:::

## The Micro-Moment Time-Line Interview (MMTLI)

Dervin's signature technique. It anchors the conversation in a specific, concrete moment — preventing the retrospective abstraction and generalisation that plague most interviews.

**Step 1 — Situating move**
: *"Tell me about a specific time when you had to [navigate X / deal with Y]. Not in general — a specific moment."*

**Step 2 — Step-through**
: *"Walk me through what happened, step by step, from the beginning."* The interviewer constructs a mental timeline of the narrative.

**Step 3 — Gap probe**
: At each step: *"At this point, what questions did you have? What was confusing? What were you trying to figure out?"*

**Step 4 — Bridge probe**
: *"What helped you with that? What did you find or use? What did you draw on?"*

**Step 5 — Outcome probe**
: *"How did that help — or not help? What were you able to do as a result? What happened next?"*

**Step 6 — Hurt probe**
: *"Was there anything that got in the way? Anything that made it harder or more confusing?"*

## Neutral Questioning

| Avoid (Non-Neutral) | Use (Neutral) | Why |
|---------------------|---------------|-----|
| "What information did you need?" | "What questions did you have at that point?" | Avoids presupposing information as the only bridge resource |
| "Did the library help you?" | "What helped you move forward?" | Avoids privileging formal information systems |
| "Was the information useful?" | "How did that help — or not help?" | Opens space for both helpful and unhelpful outcomes |
| "What topic were you researching?" | "What were you trying to do or figure out?" | Frames inquiry around the gap, not the topic |
| "Did you understand it?" | "What made sense? What remained confusing?" | Separates understanding from use |

## Analysis Protocol

| Stage | Method | What You're Looking For |
|-------|--------|------------------------|
| Transcript coding | Open coding for S-G-B-O markers; in-vivo codes for gap types | Where in the transcript do gaps, bridges, and outcomes appear? |
| Gap taxonomy | Code gaps by type (decision, barrier, spin-out, etc.) | What kinds of discontinuity are most common? |
| Bridge mapping | Map which bridge types address which gap types | Are bridges matched to gaps? Any gaps systematically under-bridged? |
| Outcome analysis | Code as: helps, hurts, new gaps; classify use types | What is the downstream effect of different bridge types? |
| Comparative analysis | Compare S-G-B-O patterns across participants/groups | What structural patterns appear across individual stories? |

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Identifying gaps in narratives:** Read transcripts and ask continuously: *"At this point, what does this person not yet know, feel, or understand that they need in order to move forward?"* The answer at each point is the gap. Mark every instance of "I didn't know," "I wasn't sure," "I was confused" — and especially every "but" or "however," which often signals gap-bridge failure.
:::

---

# Applied Practice {#applied}

## UX Research

**Traditional question:** "Do users find the navigation intuitive?"

**SMM question:** "At what moments do users encounter confusion? What do they reach for to resolve it?"

**In practice:** Conduct MMTLI-style user interviews anchored in the last time they used the product for a specific purpose. Probe for gaps (confusion, questions, frustration) and bridges (what helped, what they turned to). Design output: a gap map of the experience, annotated with bridge types. Design for gap-bridging, not feature delivery.

## Strategy

**Traditional question:** "What message should we send about the strategy?"

**SMM question:** "What gaps does our audience have in this situation — about their role, the change, the future?"

**In practice:** Gap audit through MMTLI interviews with stakeholders. Bridge inventory: what resources (formal and informal) are people already using? Outcome mapping: which bridges help, which hurt? Design communications that address *actual* gaps — not leadership's desired messages.

## Communications

**Traditional question:** "What do we need to tell them?"

**SMM question:** "What gaps do they have, and what bridges would help them cross those gaps?"

**Design principle:** Organise content by gap type, not by topic. A message organised around "decision-gap: should I adopt this?" is more effective than one organised around "here is information about the new system."

## Personal Decision-Making

**Traditional question:** "What are my options?"

**SMM question:** "What is the gap? What situation am I in? What bridges are available? What sense might I need to *unmake* first?"

**In practice:** Name the gap precisely before seeking bridges. Is it a decision-gap? A confusion-gap? A barrier-gap? Different gap types require different bridge strategies. Sense-unmaking check: what existing assumptions need dismantling before the gap can be crossed?

## Analytics & Digital Product Work

This is where I actually use SMM day-to-day. Quantitative analytics tells you *what* happened — where users dropped off, which variant won, which segment converts. SMM is the framework I reach for when I need to understand *why*. They are not competing methods; they are sequential ones.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="minimal"}
**The core problem SMM solves in analytics**

Most analytics practices measure outcomes. A funnel chart shows drop-off at step 3. A heatmap shows where users stop scrolling. But none of this tells you what gap the user was experiencing at that moment — and therefore none of it tells you what bridge to build. SMM is the diagnostic layer that sits underneath quantitative data and makes it actionable.
:::

| Analytics Signal | What Analytics Tells You | What SMM Asks Next |
|-----------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| 70% drop-off at checkout step 3 | Users are leaving at a specific point | "Walk me through the last time you were trying to complete this purchase and something stopped you. What was happening at that moment?" |
| A/B test: Variant B wins by 12% | B performs better on the target metric | "What gap were users experiencing that Variant B bridged more effectively? What bridge did B provide that A didn't?" |
| Feature adoption at 8% after launch | Users are not using the new feature | "Is the gap this feature was designed to bridge actually the gap users have? Or did we design a bridge for an assumed gap?" |
| Support ticket volume spikes after redesign | Something broke in the user experience | "What sense did users have of the old design that the redesign unmade? What new gaps did we create?" |
| Session length drops in cohort analysis | Users are spending less time engaged | "What changed in users' situations that shifted which gaps they're bringing to this product?" |

### UX Telemetry + SMM: A Combined Protocol

UX telemetry — event tracking, session replay, heatmaps — gives you behavioural data at scale. SMM gives you the interpretive framework for that data. The combination is more powerful than either alone.

1. **Instrument for gap signals.** Beyond conversion events, instrument for frustration signals — rage clicks, repeated navigation attempts, search queries within the product, back-button behaviour. These are behavioural gap-markers: the telemetry equivalent of "I was lost," "I couldn't find."

2. **Cluster users by gap-signal patterns.** Users who rage-click and back-navigate are experiencing something different from users who quietly exit. The gap taxonomy gives you a theoretically grounded way to segment: barrier gaps vs. confusion gaps vs. decision gaps produce distinct behavioural signatures in telemetry data.

3. **Conduct targeted MMTLI interviews with each cluster.** Sample 5–8 users from each behavioural cluster. The telemetry tells you *where* the gap appeared. The MMTLI tells you its *nature* and what kind of bridge users wanted.

4. **Design bridges for gap types, not for conversion metrics.** A checkout abandonment that stems from a trust gap requires a different bridge than one that stems from a logistics barrier gap. SMM diagnosis specifies what problem the solution must solve.

### Finding the Hypothesis When You Only Know the Question

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
This is a talk concept I am developing for the Amsterdam data and analytics community — for the Netherlands dbt Meetup and DDMA Digital Analytics Summit audiences.

**The core argument:** Data analytics and qualitative user research are not competing methods. They are sequential ones. Analytics surfaces the fact of a gap. SMM provides the methodology for diagnosing its nature. You cannot design a bridge without knowing the gap type. A decision gap, a confusion gap, and a barrier gap at the same funnel step require entirely different interventions.
:::

| What Analytics Gives You | What You Still Need | What SMM Provides |
|--------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Conversion rate at step 3: 31% | Why 69% leave at this specific step | Gap taxonomy for diagnosing the nature of the discontinuity |
| Session replay showing rage clicks | What the user was trying to accomplish | MMTLI protocol for surfacing the gap in its situational context |
| A/B test winner: Variant B +14% | What gap Variant B bridges more effectively | Bridge theory for explaining *why* the winning variant won |
| Cohort retention drops at week 3 | What changes in the user's situation | Situational analysis — how do sense-making conditions shift over time? |

## Practice Projects

The best way to develop SMM fluency is to apply it to familiar contexts where you already have intuitions. Each project takes 30–90 minutes and produces something usable or showable.

### P1 — Gap-Map a Funnel You Know

Take any multi-step funnel — a checkout flow, an onboarding sequence, a sign-up form. Identify the three steps with the highest drop-off. For each step, generate three hypotheses about the gap type using the taxonomy (decision, barrier, confusion, role, spin-out, washout). Then write one MMTLI-style question you would ask a user to test each hypothesis.

**Output:** A gap hypothesis map with testable questions attached to each funnel step.
**Time:** 45–60 minutes.
**SMM concept exercised:** Gap taxonomy + neutral questioning.

### P2 — Rewrite a Survey Using SMM Principles

Take any survey — NPS, post-purchase, employee engagement. Read each question and identify whether it is asking about a situation, gap, bridge, or outcome — and whether it is presupposing a bridge type. Rewrite five questions using SMM-neutral framing. Compare the original and rewritten versions: which would produce more actionable data?

**Output:** Original vs. SMM-rewritten question set with rationale.
**Time:** 30–45 minutes.
**SMM concept exercised:** Neutral questioning + S-G-B-O structure.

### P3 — Apply the MMTLI to Your Own Recent Decision

Think of a significant decision you made in the last three months. Apply the MMTLI protocol to yourself: situate it, walk through it step by step, identify every gap you encountered (by type), name every bridge you used, and trace the outcomes. Most people are surprised by how many gaps they crossed unconsciously and how diverse their bridge types were.

**Output:** Annotated personal narrative with S-G-B-O coding.
**Time:** 30 minutes writing, 15 minutes coding.
**SMM concept exercised:** Full MMTLI + gap identification + bridge typing.

### P4 — A/B Test Post-Mortem

Take any A/B test result. The standard analysis ends at "variant B won by X%." Apply SMM: What gap did each variant offer a bridge for? Was the winning variant providing a more effective bridge for the same gap, or was it bridging a different gap entirely? This reframe often reveals why a winning test still produces unexpectedly modest business results — because you measured bridge adoption, not gap resolution.

**Output:** Gap-bridge analysis of test variants; reframed hypothesis.
**Time:** 45 minutes.
**SMM concept exercised:** Bridge theory + sense-unmaking + outcome typing.

---

# Cliff Notes {#cliff}

## The Model in One Paragraph

> A person moves through time-space in a specific situation. They encounter a gap — a discontinuity in their understanding or ability to move forward. To cross the gap they construct a bridge, drawing on internal resources (memory, emotion, knowledge) and external resources (information, people, systems). The bridge produces an outcome: it helps (movement continues), hurts (movement is blocked), or creates a new gap (requiring more bridging). Sense-unmaking is the complementary practice of dismantling existing bridges when they no longer serve.

## Core Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| `SMM` | Sense-Making Methodology — Dervin's framework for studying how people construct meaning across discontinuities in time-space |
| `Situation` | The specific time-space context that constitutes sense-making — not background, but ground |
| `Gap` | The discontinuity interrupting forward movement; the engine of sense-making |
| `Bridge` | Any resource constructed to cross a gap: informational, emotional, social, procedural, cognitive |
| `Outcome / Use` | What results from bridging: helps, hurts, new gaps, and specific uses (deciding, acting, connecting) |
| `MMTLI` | Micro-Moment Time-Line Interview — Dervin's 6-step interview protocol anchored in a specific moment |
| `Neutral questioning` | Questions avoiding presupposition of gap type, bridge type, or outcome type |
| `Sense-unmaking` | Dismantling existing sense structures — required for transformation, learning, and critical thought |

## Key Principles

1. Study **gaps**, not needs
2. Study **movement**, not static states
3. Study **specific episodes**, not abstract patterns
4. Study **bridges** (any type), not content delivery
5. Study **outcomes in both directions** — helps *and* hurts
6. Always situate: specific moment, specific person, specific context
7. Design for gap-bridging, not message-sending

## Common Mistakes

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Equating bridge with information.** Information is one bridge type among many. Bridges can also be emotional, social, procedural, or drawn from memory. Privileging information replicates the transmission model inside SMM's frame.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Abstracting from situation.** Asking "what do users generally need?" rather than "what did this person need in this moment?" evacuates the methodology's explanatory power.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Ignoring sense-unmaking.** Treating SMM as purely constructive misses resistance, confusion, and transformative learning — contexts where old sense must be dismantled before new sense can be made.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Topic-based coding.** Coding by topic ("questions about pricing") rather than gap type ("decision-gap requiring comparison") collapses back into traditional information-needs categorisation.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"}
**Only probing positive outcomes.** Asking only "what helped?" misses bridge failures and hurts — often the most diagnostically valuable data.
:::

---

# Active Recall {#recall}

## Flashcards

*Click any card to reveal the answer. Both question and answer print automatically in PDF.*

```{=html}
<div class="fc-grid" id="fc-container"></div>
<script>
const cards = [
  { q: "What is the root metaphor of SMM?", a: "A person moving through time-space encounters a gap (discontinuity) and must construct a bridge to continue forward. Every element of SMM flows from this spatial-temporal image." },
  { q: "Define 'gap' in Dervin's framework.", a: "The discontinuity that interrupts forward movement — a question, confusion, barrier, dilemma, or absence. Not a failure; the engine of sense-making." },
  { q: "What is a 'bridge' and how does it differ from 'information'?", a: "A bridge is any resource constructed to cross a gap: ideas, memories, emotions, social connections, procedures, or information. Information is just one bridge type among many." },
  { q: "What are the four S-G-B-O elements?", a: "Situation (context), Gap (discontinuity), Bridge (constructed crossing), Outcome/Use (what results). The framework is recursive — outcomes create new situations." },
  { q: "What is sense-unmaking?", a: "The deliberate or necessary dismantling of existing sense structures. Required for unlearning, transformation, and critical thought. Not failure — a constitutive part of the process." },
  { q: "Why does SMM insist on specific moments?", a: "Because sense-making is always situated in a specific time-space context. General questions abstract away from the situational ground that constitutes the sense-making." },
  { q: "What is neutral questioning?", a: "A technique avoiding presupposition of gap type, bridge type, or outcome type — letting respondents name their own experience rather than conforming to researcher categories." },
  { q: "Name four gap types in Dervin's taxonomy.", a: "Decision (choosing between paths), Barrier (external obstacle), Spin-out (total disorientation), Washout (information received but unhelpful)." },
  { q: "What is the MMTLI?", a: "Micro-Moment Time-Line Interview. Six steps: situate, step-through, probe gaps, probe bridges, probe outcomes, probe hurts." },
  { q: "How does SMM's ontology differ from the transmission model?", a: "Transmission model: information exists independently as an object. SMM: information is constructed in the act of bridging — always relational, situational, never free-standing." },
  { q: "What does 'outcome' include beyond 'was the gap bridged'?", a: "Helps (forward movement), Hurts (movement blocked), New gaps, and Use types: deciding, acting, connecting, evaluating, feeling." },
  { q: "Why probe for hurts as well as helps?", a: "Bridges often fail or generate worse gaps. Asking only about successes produces asymmetric data that misrepresents how sense-making actually works." },
  { q: "What philosophical tradition most informs SMM's situatedness?", a: "Phenomenology (Schutz, Husserl) — experience is always embodied, positioned, temporal. Also constructivism (Kelly) — knowledge is constructed, not received." },
  { q: "What is the 'conduit metaphor' Dervin critiques?", a: "The view that communication is a pipeline transmitting information intact from sender to receiver. Dervin shows empirically this fails: meaning is made, not transmitted." },
  { q: "What does 'universality of procedure, uniqueness of instance' mean?", a: "Everyone sense-makes (universal procedure), but the specific sense any person makes is unique to their situation, history, and moment." },
  { q: "What is 'situation' and why is it constitutive?", a: "The specific time-space context of a person. Constitutive because it determines what counts as a gap, what bridges are available, and what outcomes are possible." },
  { q: "How does sense-unmaking relate to power?", a: "Dominant groups impose sense structures. Marginalized groups must unmake imposed sense to survive and resist. Sense-unmaking is therefore both cognitive and political." },
  { q: "What is the key unit of analysis in SMM research?", a: "The gap — the specific discontinuity experienced by a specific person in a specific situation. Not the document, the topic, or the information object." },
  { q: "Why is gap-type coding superior to topic coding?", a: "Topic coding describes content. Gap-type coding describes the nature of the discontinuity and enables structural cross-domain analysis impossible from topic categorisation." },
  { q: "What is the core design principle SMM implies for communications?", a: "Design for gap-bridging, not message-sending. Ask 'what gaps does our audience have?' not 'what do we want to tell them?'" },
];

const container = document.getElementById('fc-container');
cards.forEach((card, i) => {
  const fc = document.createElement('div');
  fc.className = 'fc';
  fc.innerHTML = `
    <div class="fc-inner">
      <div class="fc-front">
        <div>
          <div class="fc-num">Card ${String(i+1).padStart(2,'0')}</div>
          <div class="fc-q">${card.q}</div>
        </div>
        <div class="fc-hint">click to reveal →</div>
      </div>
      <div class="fc-back">
        <div class="fc-a">${card.a}</div>
        <div class="fc-hint">← click to flip back</div>
      </div>
    </div>`;
  fc.addEventListener('click', () => fc.classList.toggle('flipped'));
  container.appendChild(fc);
});
</script>
```

## Test Questions

*Click each question to reveal the answer.*

```{=html}
<details class="tq">
  <summary><div class="tq-num">1</div>A UX researcher says: "Users need information about pricing, features, and comparisons." What would Dervin critique and how would she reframe this?</summary>
  <div class="tq-body">This replicates topic-based, transmission-model thinking. Dervin would critique: (1) "information needs" abstracts from situation; (2) it presupposes information as the only bridge; (3) it describes content rather than gap type. SMM reframe: conduct MMTLI interviews about specific moments — identify gap types (decision, confusion, barrier), map bridge attempts (including non-informational ones), analyse outcomes. "Pricing information need" becomes: "decision-gap requiring comparison to choose between plans — bridges attempted: pricing page scan (hurt: confusing), social query (helped), support call (mixed outcome)."</div>
</details>

<details class="tq">
  <summary><div class="tq-num">2</div>Explain why 'outcome' in SMM is more analytically powerful than 'satisfaction' or 'usability'.</summary>
  <div class="tq-body">Satisfaction/usability measure reaction to an artifact in isolation. SMM outcome is tied to the specific gap: (1) Helps — what movement was enabled, how; (2) Hurts — how the bridge failed or worsened the situation; (3) New gaps — secondary discontinuities created; (4) Use types — what the person actually did differently. A highly "satisfying" document may bridge no actual gaps; a frustrating search experience may ultimately help enormously. SMM makes effectiveness situated and causal, not preferential.</div>
</details>

<details class="tq">
  <summary><div class="tq-num">3</div>A communications director says: "We sent detailed strategy communications but employees are still confused." Apply SMM to diagnose this.</summary>
  <div class="tq-body">The director applied a transmission model: encode → send → assume comprehension. SMM diagnosis: employees' actual gaps (What does this mean for my role? / Why is this better than before? / Who can I trust?) were never diagnosed. The communication was designed around what leadership wanted to transmit, not around gap types employees actually have. Fix: MMTLI-style interviews or open-ended surveys asking about specific moments of uncertainty. Identify gap types. Design communications as bridges for those gaps — organised by gap, not by leadership's topic preferences.</div>
</details>

<details class="tq">
  <summary><div class="tq-num">4</div>What problem does SMM's sense-unmaking concept create for knowledge management systems?</summary>
  <div class="tq-body">KM systems assume knowledge is a capturable, retrievable object — a transmission model. Sense-unmaking reveals two problems: (1) Knowledge is a bridge constructed for a specific gap in a specific situation — a document that bridges one gap may fail entirely in another context; (2) Transformation requires dismantling existing "knowledge," which a deposit-and-retrieve system cannot facilitate. SMM-informed KM would index resources by gap type, surface assumption-challenging materials, and build processes for questioning existing organisational sense.</div>
</details>

<details class="tq">
  <summary><div class="tq-num">5</div>Design an SMM study of how expats navigate healthcare in a new country.</summary>
  <div class="tq-body">MMTLI interviews anchored in a specific recent healthcare-seeking moment. Step through from initial need to resolution. Gap probes at each step: role gaps (how to register?), decision gaps (which specialist?), barrier gaps (language, system structure), trust gaps (who is reliable here?). Bridge analysis: internet searches, expat communities, calling home, GP visits, trial and error. Output: a gap map organised by gap type, enabling communications and services to bridge actual gaps — not assumed "information needs."</div>
</details>
```

## Scenario Applications

```{=html}
<div class="scenario">
  <div class="scenario-head">
    <span class="scenario-num">01</span>
    <div><div class="scenario-domain">UX Research</div><div class="scenario-title">Mobile banking — 40% transfer abandonment</div></div>
  </div>
  <div class="scenario-grid">
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Situation</span><p>First-time international transfer attempt on a new mobile app.</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Gap(s)</span><p>Procedural (how to do this?), decision (which transfer type?), barrier (IBAN not to hand), anxiety gap (is this safe?).</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Bridge(s)</span><p>Abandons app; calls bank; searches online; asks a friend; tries again with documents.</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Outcome(s)</span><p>Phone call helps but app trust eroded. New gap: "Why didn't the app tell me what I'd need beforehand?"</p></div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="scenario">
  <div class="scenario-head">
    <span class="scenario-num">02</span>
    <div><div class="scenario-domain">Expat Healthcare</div><div class="scenario-title">Navigating Dutch GP registration as a new arrival</div></div>
  </div>
  <div class="scenario-grid">
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Situation</span><p>New expat in the Netherlands; needs a GP; no prior knowledge of how huisarts system works.</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Gap(s)</span><p>Role gap (what am I supposed to do?), procedure gap (how does registration work?), trust gap (which GP will communicate in English?), barrier gap (practice lists may be full).</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Bridge(s)</span><p>Expat forums, municipality website, colleague advice, trial calls to practices, insurance company FAQ.</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Outcome(s)</span><p>Forums help most. Official sources too generic. New gap after registration: how does the referral system work?</p></div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="scenario">
  <div class="scenario-head">
    <span class="scenario-num">03</span>
    <div><div class="scenario-domain">Strategy</div><div class="scenario-title">Post-merger integration — employee resistance</div></div>
  </div>
  <div class="scenario-grid">
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Situation</span><p>Employee receiving strategy communications after merger, facing changed teams and leadership.</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Gap(s)</span><p>Role gap (what does this mean for my job?), decision gap (should I look elsewhere?), trust gap (who can I believe?), problematic gap (cultural fit feels wrong).</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Bridge(s)</span><p>Informal peer conversations, reading between lines, LinkedIn scanning, cautious manager conversations.</p></div>
    <div class="scenario-cell"><span class="scenario-cell-label">Outcome(s)</span><p>Formal communications bridge none of the actual gaps. Informal network is the most effective bridge.</p></div>
  </div>
</div>
```

---

# Comparative Analysis {#compare}

```{=html}
<table class="compare">
<thead><tr><th>Framework</th><th>Primary Unit</th><th>Time Direction</th><th>Method Prescribed?</th><th>Key Difference from SMM</th><th>Best Integration</th></tr></thead>
<tbody>
<tr><td>Weickian Sensemaking</td><td>Organisation / collective</td><td>Retrospective</td><td>No — descriptive only</td><td>Less methodologically specific; organisational not individual level</td><td>Use Weick for organisational-level context (situation); Dervin for individual-level gap-bridging within it</td></tr>
<tr><td>Design Thinking</td><td>Problem-solution cycle</td><td>Prospective</td><td>Partially (stages, not interview method)</td><td>Generative not diagnostic; "empathy" framing can be shallow</td><td>SMM for rigorous gap diagnosis in DT phases 1–2; DT ideation tools to design bridges for SMM-surfaced gaps</td></tr>
<tr><td>User Journey Mapping</td><td>Touchpoints across timeline</td><td>Both</td><td>No</td><td>Often persona-based, not grounded in specific moments; atheoretical</td><td>Use SMM interviews to generate empirically grounded data that journey maps then visualise</td></tr>
<tr><td>Systems Thinking</td><td>Feedback loops / system structure</td><td>Both</td><td>No</td><td>Macro not micro; individual agency under-theorised</td><td>Systems Thinking for why gaps are endemic; SMM for how individuals experience and navigate them</td></tr>
<tr><td>Ethnographic Interviewing</td><td>Cultural practice / thick description</td><td>Synchronic</td><td>Emergent, not prescribed</td><td>Richer cultural context; weaker cross-case comparability</td><td>Ethnographic immersion for situational context; MMTLI for systematic gap-bridge-outcome mapping within that context</td></tr>
</tbody>
</table>
```

---

# Ethical Projects {#ethics}

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
**Core ethical principle:** SMM positions participants as active sense-makers, not informants. Research design must protect their ability to make sense of the research process itself — informed consent, power transparency, and benefit reciprocity are structural requirements, not procedural formalities.
:::

```{=html}
<div class="ethics-card">
  <div class="ethics-head">
    <strong>01 · Healthcare Navigation Study</strong>
    <p style="font-size:.85rem;color:#666;margin:.3rem 0 0;">Interview patients about moments of uncertainty navigating care systems — especially relevant in cross-national or expat contexts.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="ethics-grid">
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-risk">Ethical Risks</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Recall may surface trauma or fear — prepare de-escalation and local crisis referral options</li>
        <li>Health data carries legal and ethical weight — collect only what illuminates gap types</li>
        <li>Power asymmetry: participants may feel obligated to share more than comfortable</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-mit">Mitigations</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Full informed consent with explicit right to stop at any point; no deception</li>
        <li>Anonymise at analysis stage; destroy raw audio after coding</li>
        <li>Co-design consent forms with patient advocates from the community</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="ethics-card">
  <div class="ethics-head">
    <strong>02 · Expat Cultural Adaptation Study</strong>
    <p style="font-size:.85rem;color:#666;margin:.3rem 0 0;">Map sense-making gaps as people navigate new national systems — healthcare, education, employment, social norms.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="ethics-grid">
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-risk">Ethical Risks</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Language barriers may distort gap reporting (gaps in expressing gaps)</li>
        <li>Cultural norms around disclosure vary; what counts as a "gap" may differ cross-culturally</li>
        <li>Researcher positionality: expat researcher may over-identify; non-expat may misread</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-mit">Mitigations</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Interview in participant's language of comfort; use bilingual co-researchers</li>
        <li>Pilot MMTLI instrument with diverse cultural consultants before deployment</li>
        <li>Maintain a reflexive research journal; include positionality statement in publications</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="ethics-card">
  <div class="ethics-head">
    <strong>03 · Workplace Onboarding Friction Map</strong>
    <p style="font-size:.85rem;color:#666;margin:.3rem 0 0;">Identify new employee sense-making gaps during onboarding — especially role-gaps and systemic barrier-gaps.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="ethics-grid">
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-risk">Ethical Risks</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Managerial misuse: findings used to evaluate rather than support employees</li>
        <li>Retaliation risk if negative experiences are traceable to individuals</li>
        <li>Organisational inertia: data collected but never acted on (ethical failure of omission)</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-mit">Mitigations</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Contractually establish: data is not used for performance review</li>
        <li>Aggregate all reporting; individuals never identified in outputs</li>
        <li>Establish change commitment before beginning; co-design action plan with HR</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="ethics-card">
  <div class="ethics-head">
    <strong>04 · Community Crisis Communication Review</strong>
    <p style="font-size:.85rem;color:#666;margin:.3rem 0 0;">Analyse how people bridged uncertainty during emergencies — natural disasters, public health crises, civil unrest.</p>
  </div>
  <div class="ethics-grid">
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-risk">Ethical Risks</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Re-traumatisation through narrative recall of crisis moments</li>
        <li>Community distrust if research extracts without reciprocating</li>
        <li>Over-generalisation from crisis-state to normal conditions</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
    <div class="ethics-cell">
      <span class="ethics-label-mit">Mitigations</span>
      <ul>
        <li>Train interviewers in trauma-informed questioning; provide resource sheets</li>
        <li>Return findings to community in accessible form; involve them in interpretation</li>
        <li>Contextualise findings explicitly; label crisis-specific limitations</li>
      </ul>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

### Universal Ethical Commitments in SMM Research

| Principle | What It Requires in SMM Practice |
|-----------|----------------------------------|
| Informed consent as ongoing | Because MMTLI interviews follow emergent narratives into unpredictable territory, consent must be renewable as sensitive material emerges — not only at the outset |
| Reciprocity | Participants share sense-making moments; researchers owe them: findings in accessible form, co-interpretation opportunities, actionable outputs |
| Power transparency | MMTLI positions the researcher as gap-mapper; participants may feel their sense-making is being evaluated. Explicitly de-hierarchise the interview dynamic |
| Trauma sensitivity | Gap probes about crisis, illness, or institutional failure may surface trauma. Train interviewers in trauma-informed questioning; have referral resources available |
| Sense-unmaking ethics | If research design challenges participants' existing worldviews (sense-unmaking), this must be consensual and supported — not imposed in the name of critical method |
| Data minimisation | SMM's richness tempts over-collection. Collect only what illuminates gap types and bridge patterns for your specific research question |

---

# Mastery Plan {#plan}

## 7-Day Study Protocol

```{=html}
<div class="day-grid">
  <div class="day-card">
    <div class="day-head"><span class="day-n">1</span><span class="day-name">Mon</span></div>
    <div class="day-body">
      <div class="day-theme">Foundations</div>
      <div class="day-task">Read Core Class in full</div>
      <div class="day-task">Memorise 7 assumptions by number</div>
      <div class="day-task">Read Dervin (1992)</div>
      <div class="day-task">Write: what does 'constructed information' mean?</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="day-card">
    <div class="day-head"><span class="day-n">2</span><span class="day-name">Tue</span></div>
    <div class="day-body">
      <div class="day-theme">Core Concepts</div>
      <div class="day-task">Master S-G-B-O from memory</div>
      <div class="day-task">Reproduce diagrams by hand</div>
      <div class="day-task">Memorise gap taxonomy</div>
      <div class="day-task">Drill flashcards 1–10</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="day-card">
    <div class="day-head"><span class="day-n">3</span><span class="day-name">Wed</span></div>
    <div class="day-body">
      <div class="day-theme">Methodology</div>
      <div class="day-task">Study MMTLI protocol</div>
      <div class="day-task">Rewrite 5 non-neutral Qs</div>
      <div class="day-task">Memorise analysis stages</div>
      <div class="day-task">Drill flashcards 11–20</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="day-card">
    <div class="day-head"><span class="day-n">4</span><span class="day-name">Thu</span></div>
    <div class="day-body">
      <div class="day-theme">Live Practice</div>
      <div class="day-task">Self-MMTLI on a recent decision</div>
      <div class="day-task">Code your own transcript</div>
      <div class="day-task">Identify gap types</div>
      <div class="day-task">What did sense-unmaking require?</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="day-card">
    <div class="day-head"><span class="day-n">5</span><span class="day-name">Fri</span></div>
    <div class="day-body">
      <div class="day-theme">Applied Domain</div>
      <div class="day-task">Pick one domain for your work</div>
      <div class="day-task">Design an SMM research plan</div>
      <div class="day-task">Write 8 MMTLI questions</div>
      <div class="day-task">Answer test Qs 1–3</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="day-card">
    <div class="day-head"><span class="day-n">6</span><span class="day-name">Sat</span></div>
    <div class="day-body">
      <div class="day-theme">Comparisons</div>
      <div class="day-task">Work through Sec 7</div>
      <div class="day-task">Map against frameworks you use</div>
      <div class="day-task">Where would SMM change past work?</div>
      <div class="day-task">Answer test Qs 4–5</div>
    </div>
  </div>
  <div class="day-card">
    <div class="day-head"><span class="day-n">7</span><span class="day-name">Sun</span></div>
    <div class="day-body">
      <div class="day-theme">Integration</div>
      <div class="day-task">Write full S-G-B-O from memory</div>
      <div class="day-task">Write one-page SMM summary</div>
      <div class="day-task">Work through all scenarios</div>
      <div class="day-task">Name one real project to apply</div>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

## Daily Practice Loop

Each day, spend 5 minutes on this ritual:

1. **Recall** the S-G-B-O framework from memory. Write the gap taxonomy unaided.
2. **Apply**: identify one gap you encountered today. Name its type.
3. **Bridge audit**: what did you reach for? Was it informational, emotional, social, procedural?
4. **Outcome trace**: did the bridge help, hurt, or create a new gap?
5. **Sense-unmaking check**: did you need to dismantle an existing assumption first?

## Primary Sources

| Work | Why Read It |
|------|-------------|
| Dervin (1983) — "Information as a User Construct" | Foundational statement — introduces gap-bridge metaphor; critique of transmission model |
| Dervin (1992) — "From the Mind's Eye of the User" | **Best single overview** — full S-G-B-O articulation; methodological implications. Start here. |
| Dervin & Nilan (1986) — *Annual Review of Information Science* | Landmark paradigm critique — establishes the shift from topic-based to gap-based research |
| Dervin (1998) — "Sense-Making Theory and Practice" | Mature statement — introduces sense-unmaking; feminist and power dimensions |
| Dervin (1999) — "On Studying Information Seeking Methodologically" | Deepest methodological discussion — best source on MMTLI design and analysis |

---

# SMM Question Rewriter {#rewriter}

Paste any survey, UX interview, or research question and this tool rewrites it using Sense-Making principles — surfacing the gap, removing bridge presuppositions, and anchoring in a specific moment rather than a general pattern.

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
**When to use this:** Survey design before fieldwork. UX interview guide review. A/B test hypothesis sharpening. Anywhere you are about to ask a human a question and want to make sure you are asking about their gap rather than your assumed topic.
:::

```{=html}
<div style="background:#fff;border:1px solid #d4d0c8;border-radius:10px;padding:28px;margin:24px 0;">

  <div style="margin-bottom:16px;">
    <label style="display:block;font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace;font-size:.65rem;letter-spacing:.15em;text-transform:uppercase;color:#0d6e56;margin-bottom:8px;">Your question</label>
    <textarea id="rw-input" placeholder="e.g. 'Did you find the information you were looking for?' or 'How satisfied were you with the checkout process?'" style="width:100%;min-height:88px;border:1px solid #d4d0c8;border-radius:6px;padding:12px 14px;font-family:'Source Serif 4',Georgia,serif;font-size:14px;line-height:1.6;resize:vertical;color:#111;outline:none;background:#fffef7;"></textarea>
  </div>

  <div style="margin-bottom:20px;">
    <label style="display:block;font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace;font-size:.65rem;letter-spacing:.15em;text-transform:uppercase;color:#0d6e56;margin-bottom:8px;">Context</label>
    <div style="display:flex;gap:8px;flex-wrap:wrap;" id="ctx-buttons">
      <button class="rw-ctx active" onclick="setCtx(this,'UX research interview')">UX Interview</button>
      <button class="rw-ctx" onclick="setCtx(this,'customer survey')">Survey</button>
      <button class="rw-ctx" onclick="setCtx(this,'A/B test hypothesis')">A/B Testing</button>
      <button class="rw-ctx" onclick="setCtx(this,'analytics investigation')">Analytics</button>
      <button class="rw-ctx" onclick="setCtx(this,'employee or stakeholder interview')">Stakeholder</button>
      <button class="rw-ctx" onclick="setCtx(this,'general qualitative research')">General</button>
    </div>
  </div>

  <button id="rw-btn" onclick="rewriteQuestion()" style="background:#0d6e56;color:#fff;border:none;border-radius:6px;padding:11px 24px;font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace;font-size:.7rem;font-weight:500;letter-spacing:.1em;cursor:pointer;text-transform:uppercase;">Rewrite with SMM →</button>

  <div id="rw-loading" style="display:none;margin-top:20px;padding:14px 16px;background:#f7f9f8;border-left:3px solid #0d6e56;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace;font-size:.75rem;color:#0d6e56;">Analysing gap structure and rewriting...</div>
  <div id="rw-result" style="display:none;margin-top:20px;"></div>
  <div id="rw-error" style="display:none;margin-top:20px;padding:14px 16px;background:#fdf2ef;border-left:3px solid #b94a28;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;font-family:'JetBrains Mono',monospace;font-size:.75rem;color:#b94a28;"></div>

</div>

<style>
.rw-ctx {
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
  font-size: .65rem;
  letter-spacing: .08em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  padding: 5px 12px;
  border-radius: 4px;
  border: 1px solid #d4d0c8;
  background: #fff;
  color: #888;
  cursor: pointer;
  transition: all .12s;
}
.rw-ctx.active, .rw-ctx:hover {
  background: #f0f7f4;
  border-color: #0d6e56;
  color: #0d6e56;
}
.rw-block {
  background: #f7f9f8;
  border: 1px solid #d4d0c8;
  border-radius: 8px;
  padding: 14px 16px;
  margin-bottom: 12px;
}
.rw-label {
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
  font-size: .6rem;
  letter-spacing: .15em;
  text-transform: uppercase;
  color: #0d6e56;
  margin-bottom: 5px;
  display: block;
}
.rw-text {
  font-size: 14px;
  color: #333;
  line-height: 1.65;
}
.rw-badge {
  display: inline-block;
  background: #f0f7f4;
  color: #0d6e56;
  font-family: 'JetBrains Mono', monospace;
  font-size: .6rem;
  letter-spacing: .08em;
  padding: 2px 8px;
  border-radius: 3px;
  border: 1px solid #0d6e56;
  margin-left: 8px;
}
</style>

<script>
let rwCtx = 'UX research interview';
function setCtx(btn, ctx) {
  document.querySelectorAll('.rw-ctx').forEach(b => b.classList.remove('active'));
  btn.classList.add('active');
  rwCtx = ctx;
}
async function rewriteQuestion() {
  const input = document.getElementById('rw-input').value.trim();
  if (!input) return;
  const btn = document.getElementById('rw-btn');
  const loading = document.getElementById('rw-loading');
  const result = document.getElementById('rw-result');
  const error = document.getElementById('rw-error');
  btn.disabled = true; btn.textContent = 'Rewriting...';
  loading.style.display = 'block'; result.style.display = 'none'; error.style.display = 'none';
  try {
    const res = await fetch('https://api.anthropic.com/v1/messages', {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
      body: JSON.stringify({
        model: 'claude-sonnet-4-20250514',
        max_tokens: 1000,
        system: `You are an expert in Brenda Dervin's Sense-Making Methodology. Rewrite a question from ${rwCtx} using SMM principles: ask about a specific moment not general patterns; surface the gap not topics or satisfaction; do not presuppose bridge type; use neutral questioning; frame around verbs not nouns. Return ONLY valid JSON: {"diagnosis":"What is wrong with the original (2-3 sentences)","rewritten":"The SMM-rewritten question","gapType":"Gap type this surfaces (Decision/Barrier/Confusion/Role/Spin-out/Washout/Multiple)","explanation":"Why this produces better data (2-3 sentences)"}`,
        messages: [{ role: 'user', content: `Original question: "${input}"` }]
      })
    });
    const data = await res.json();
    const parsed = JSON.parse(data.content[0].text.replace(/```json|```/g,'').trim());
    result.innerHTML = `
      <div class="rw-block"><div class="rw-label">What's wrong with the original</div><div class="rw-text">${parsed.diagnosis}</div></div>
      <div class="rw-block" style="background:#f0f7f4;border-color:#0d6e56;"><div class="rw-label">SMM rewrite <span class="rw-badge">${parsed.gapType} gap</span></div><div class="rw-text" style="font-style:italic;">"${parsed.rewritten}"</div></div>
      <div class="rw-block"><div class="rw-label">Why this produces better data</div><div class="rw-text">${parsed.explanation}</div></div>`;
    result.style.display = 'block';
  } catch(err) {
    error.textContent = 'Something went wrong: ' + (err.message || 'API error — check network connection.');
    error.style.display = 'block';
  } finally {
    loading.style.display = 'none'; btn.disabled = false; btn.textContent = 'Rewrite with SMM →';
  }
}
</script>
```

What makes an SMM question different:

| Original Question | SMM Rewrite | What Changed |
|------------------|-------------|--------------|
| "Did you find the information you needed?" | "Tell me about a moment when you weren't sure what to do next. What were you trying to figure out?" | Removes "information" assumption; asks about a specific moment; opens gap to any bridge type |
| "How satisfied were you with checkout?" | "Was there any point during checkout where something stopped you or made you uncertain? What happened?" | Moves from satisfaction rating to gap identification; surfaces actual friction |
| "What features do you want?" | "Think about the last time you got stuck in this product. What were you trying to do, and what would have helped you get past that moment?" | Replaces abstract preference with specific gap-bridging need |

---

# About {#about}

## Why I Built This

Dervin's Sense-Making Methodology is the qualitative counterpart to the quantitative analytics work I do every day. The telemetry tells me *what* happened. SMM gives me the framework for understanding *why* — what gap the user was experiencing, what bridge they were trying to construct, and why the product did or didn't help them across it.

Most analytics practices are measurement frameworks. Conversion rate, session length, feature adoption, cohort retention. These are all *outcomes* in SMM's framework. They tell you that a gap existed and whether a bridge was crossed. They do not tell you what the gap was, what kind of bridge users were looking for, or why the bridge worked for some users and not others.

::: {.callout-important appearance="minimal"}
**The moment I reach for SMM**

Every time I have a quantitative finding I cannot explain. A segment that converts at half the rate of every other segment. A feature that 91% of users never touch despite being prominently placed. An A/B test that wins statistically but produces no downstream business impact. These are situations where telemetry has identified a gap but cannot diagnose its nature. SMM is the diagnostic framework for moving from "something is happening here" to "here is what the user is experiencing and what kind of bridge would actually help."
:::

## Where This Shows Up in My Work

**Measurement planning.** Before any analytics implementation, I use the S-G-B-O framework to define what we are actually trying to measure. Not "we want to track page views" — but "users are in situation X, they encounter gap Y, what bridge does the product offer, and what outcome do we want to measure?" This produces a measurement plan that is theoretically grounded, not just technically complete.

**Interview design for UX research.** When I design research interviews alongside quantitative work, I use MMTLI structure. The quantitative data tells me *where* to look; the MMTLI tells me *how to ask* about what I find there. The neutral questioning technique is particularly important in analytics contexts — it is easy to contaminate an interview by presupposing that "information" was the bridge users were looking for.

**Explaining why winning A/B tests sometimes don't move the business needle.** Variant B won because it better bridged the gap users had at that step. But if the real business problem is that users are arriving with the wrong gap entirely — a problem that no variant in the test addressed — then all you have done is optimise the bridge for the wrong discontinuity. SMM provides the language for this diagnosis.

**Sense-unmaking in data culture work.** When I build analytics infrastructure at organisations that have been operating without it, the hardest part is never technical. It is dismantling the existing sense structures that leaders have built about how their business works — usually based on incomplete or distorted data. Dervin's sense-unmaking concept is exactly what describes this: before new sense can be made, old sense must be deliberately dismantled.

## About the Author

**Frederic Labadie** is a senior data analyst and analytics architect based in Amsterdam. Primary stack: BigQuery, GA4, dbt, Amplitude, GTM (client + server-side). SMM is the qualitative complement to quantitative analytics work — the framework for diagnosing gap types when the numbers surface a problem but can't explain its nature.

[LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/in/fredericlabadie) · [GitHub](https://github.com/fredericlabadie) · [Writers Room](https://writersroom.fredericlabadie.com) · [VibeReader](https://vibereader.fredericlabadie.com)

---

# Fieldwork Template {#fieldwork}

*Print this section separately for use during interviews and observations. Use one sheet per episode.*

```{=html}
<div class="field-box">
  <div class="field-row">
    <span class="field-label">Date / Context</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" placeholder="Date, setting, participant code..." />
  </div>

  <div class="field-row">
    <span class="field-label">Situation</span>
    <span class="field-prompt">What was happening? What was the person's time-space context?</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
  </div>

  <div class="field-row">
    <span class="field-label">Gap(s)</span>
    <span class="field-prompt">What interrupted forward movement? Type: Decision / Barrier / Confusion / Role / Spin-out / Washout</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" placeholder="Gap 1:" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" placeholder="Gap 2:" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" placeholder="Gap 3:" />
  </div>

  <div class="field-row">
    <span class="field-label">Bridge(s)</span>
    <span class="field-prompt">What was constructed to cross the gap? Type: Informational / Emotional / Social / Procedural / Cognitive</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" placeholder="Bridge 1:" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" placeholder="Bridge 2:" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" placeholder="Bridge 3:" />
  </div>

  <div class="field-row">
    <span class="field-label">Outcome(s)</span>
    <span class="field-prompt">What changed? Circle: Helped / Hurt / New gap. Describe use (decided, acted, connected, reframed...):</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
  </div>

  <div class="field-row">
    <span class="field-label">Hurts / Failures</span>
    <span class="field-prompt">What failed? What got in the way? What made it worse?</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
  </div>

  <div class="field-row">
    <span class="field-label">Sense-Unmaking Note</span>
    <span class="field-prompt">Was any existing sense dismantled in the process? What was unlearned or destabilised?</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
  </div>

  <div class="field-row" style="margin-bottom:0;">
    <span class="field-label">Analyst Notes</span>
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
    <input class="field-line" type="text" />
  </div>
</div>
```

---

*Dervin, B. (1983, 1992, 1998, 1999). Core SMM texts. See Section 9 for full reading list.*
